sections.heading: AWS PrivateLink
sections.body:
When you enable PrivateLink in AWS (Amazon Web Services) and private
endpoints in Atlas, AWS (Amazon Web Services) and Atlas create the following
resources to support secure connections over VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s:

| Resource                 | Creator                                      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Private endpoint service | Atlas                                        | A collection of private endpoint resources within your Atlas VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) that places clusters within a region behind a network load balancer. AWS (Amazon Web Services) refers to the endpoint service as the VPC endpoint service.                     |
| Interface endpoint       | AWS (Amazon Web Services)                    | AWS (Amazon Web Services) VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) endpoint with a private IP address that sends traffic to the private endpoint service over AWS (Amazon Web Services) PrivateLink. AWS (Amazon Web Services) refers to the interface endpoint as the VPC endpoint. |
| Private endpoint         | AWS (Amazon Web Services) and Atlas together | A generic term for the private connection established between Atlas and your cloud provider. For AWS (Amazon Web Services), that connection is established using PrivateLink.                                                                                           |

AWS PrivateLink

![Image showing how AWS PrivateLink establishes connections from your application VPC to resources in the |service| VPC.](/images/atlas-aws-privatelink.svg)

AWS Direct Connect

![Image showing how you can use |aws| Direct Connect to establish a transitive connection from your data center to a peered |aws| VPC to resources in the |service| VPC using |aws| Direct Connect.](/images/atlas-aws-privatelink-directconnect.svg)

Connections to Atlas clusters using private
endpoints offer the following advantages over other network
access management options:

- Connections using private endpoints are one-way. Atlas
  VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s can't initiate connections back to your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s. This
  ensures your perceived network trust boundary is not extended.

- Connections to private endpoints within your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) can be made
  transitively from:

  - Another VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peered to the private endpoint-connected VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).

  - An on-premises data center connected with [DirectConnect](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect.html)
    to the private endpoint-connected VPC (Virtual Private Cloud). This enables you to
    connect to Atlas directly from your on-premises data
    center without adding public IP addresses to the Atlas
    IP access list.

You can also set up private endpoints for your Online Archive. To
learn more, see [Set Up a Private Endpoint for Online Archives](oa-config-private-endpoint).

sections.heading: Azure Private Link
sections.body:
When you enable this feature, Azure (Microsoft Azure) and Atlas create
the following resources to support secure connections over
VNets:

| Resource                 | Creator                                    | Description                                                                                                                                                                  |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Private endpoint service | Atlas                                      | A collection of private endpoint resources within your Atlas VNet that places clusters within a region behind a network load balancer.                                       |
| Private endpoint         | Azure (Microsoft Azure) and Atlas together | A generic term for the private connection established between Atlas and your cloud provider. For Azure (Microsoft Azure), that connection is established using Private Link. |

Atlas creates a Private
Link service and places clusters within a region behind a
load balancer in the Atlas VNet.

Then you create resources that establish a one-way
connection from your VNet to the [Private Link
service](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-link-service-overview) in
the Atlas VNet using a private endpoint. The Private
Link service routes traffic to the load balancer that
fronts the clusters in the Atlas VNet.

![Diagram showing how Azure Private Link establishes connections from your application to resources in the |service| VNet.](/images/atlas-azure-privatelink.png)

Connections to Atlas clusters using private
endpoints offer the following advantages over other network access
management options:

- Connections using private endpoints are one-way. Atlas
  VNets can't initiate connections back to your VNets. This
  ensures your perceived network trust boundary is not extended.

- Connections to private endpoints within your VNet can be
  made transitively from:

  - Another VNet peered to the private endpoint-connected VNet.

  - An on-premises data center connected with
    [ExpressRoute](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/expressroute/)
    to the private endpoint-connected VNet. This enables
    you to connect to Atlas directly from your
    on-premises data center without adding public IP
    addresses to the Atlas IP access list.

You can also set up private endpoints for your Online Archive. To
learn more, see [Set Up a Private Endpoint for Online Archives](oa-config-private-endpoint).

sections.heading: GCP Private Service Connect
sections.body:
When you enable this feature, Google Cloud and Atlas create
the following resources to support secure connections over
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s:

| Resource                 | Creator                         | Description                                                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Private endpoint service | Atlas                           | A collection of private endpoint resources within your Atlas VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) that places clusters within a region behind network load balancers.                     |
| Private endpoints        | Google Cloud and Atlas together | A generic term for the private connections established between Atlas and your cloud provider. For Google Cloud, those connections are established using Private Service Connect. |

When you enable GCP Private Service Connect in Google Cloud, Atlas
creates a private endpoint service using a [service attachment](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-service-connect#service-attachments)
and a load balancer.

Next, you create resources that establish a one-way
connection from your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) to the private endpoint service
in Atlas using a private endpoint. The private endpoint
service routes traffic to the load balancer for the
clusters in the Atlas VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).

One Cluster, One Region

The following diagram shows how GCP Private Service Connect
establishes connections when you have one cluster
in one region.

![Image showing how GCP Private Service Connect establishes connections from your application VPC to resources in the |service| VPC when you have one cluster in one region.](/images/google-psc-diagram-new.svg)

Multiple Clusters, One Region

The following diagram shows how GCP Private Service Connect
establishes connections when you have two
clusters in one region.

![Image showing how GCP Private Service Connect establishes connections from your application VPC to resources in the |service| VPC when you have two clusters in one region.](/images/google-psc-diagram-two-clusters-new.svg)

One Cluster, Multiple Regions

The following diagram shows how GCP Private Service Connect
establishes connections when you have one cluster
in multiple regions.

![Image showing how GCP Private Service Connect establishes connections from your application VPC to resources in the |service| VPC when you have one cluster in multiple regions.](/images/google-psc-diagram-multiple-regions-new.svg)

sections.heading: Legacy Private Endpoints Support
sections.body:
> **Note:** Port Mapping Architecture is the preferred architecture for new private
> endpoints in Google Cloud.

Legacy private endpoints without port mapping are still supported for existing
Google Cloud clusters with private endpoints created before the release of GCP Private Service Connect port mapping,
but you cannot create additional legacy endpoint services. However, you can
continue to use existing legacy endpoints with full functionality,
including adding and removing IP addresses from the access list,
and connecting to clusters using the legacy private endpoint connection string.

sections.heading: Limatations and Considerations
sections.body:
- For multi-region deployments, you cannot combine legacy and port mapped
  endpoint services. You must create a port mapped endpoint service in each
  region in order for port mapped connection strings to work correctly.
  This limitation does not apply if you have a regionalized private endpoint.

- For multi-cloud clusters, if both legacy endpoint and port mapped endpoint services exist for a given region,
  you must delete the legacy endpoint service before you can connect to the port mapped endpoint service.

sections.heading: Converting Legacy Private Endpoints to use Port Mapping Architecture
sections.body:
You can [create new private endpoints](private-endpoints-create) for a given region using GCP Private Service Connect port mapping
before deleting the legacy private endpoints for clusters in that region.
After you create a new private endpoint and use the newly generated
connection string in your application, [confirm that the new private endpoint is working](gcp-private-endpoints-verify) before deleting the legacy private endpoint.

> **Note:** Connection strings for legacy private endpoints differ from port mapping connection strings.
> Specifically, legacy private endpoint connection strings include
> a `pl-[index]` pattern, whereas new connection strings include a `psc-[index]` pattern.
>
> **Multi-Cloud Considerations**
> If you have a multi-cloud cluster connection strings must use the `pl-[index]`
> pattern, even if you have port mapped private endpoints in the Google Cloud region.
> For example, if you have a GCP-only cluster in Region A with port-mapping uses clusterName-psc-0.
> However, after adding an AWS node and converting to a multi-cloud architecture, you must update
> the connection string to use the `clusterName-pl-0` pattern.

sections.heading: Example API Request to Create a GCP Private Service Connect Port Mapping Private Endpoint
sections.body:
> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

```Shell
curl -4 -i --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
  --request POST \
  --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2025-03-12+json" \
  --data '{"providerName": "GCP", "region": "CENTRAL_US", "portMappingEnabled": true}' \
  "https://mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/<group-id>/privateEndpoint/endpointService"
```

> **Note:** To learn more about recommended configurations for multi-region setups,
> see [Multi-Region Deployment Paradigm](atlas/architecture/current/deployment-paradigms/multi-region).

To ensure the availability of resources for both current
and future clusters, Atlas performs the following
actions when you enable this feature:

Connections to Atlas clusters using private endpoints
offer the following advantages over other network access
management options:

- Connections using private endpoints are one-way. Atlas
  VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s can't initiate connections back to your Google Cloud
  VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s. This ensures your perceived network trust
  boundary is not extended.

- You can connect to private endpoints within your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) transitively from:

  - Another VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peered to the private endpoint-connected VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).

  - An on-premises data center connected with Google Cloud VPN
    to the private endpoint-connected VPC (Virtual Private Cloud). This enables you to
    connect to Atlas directly from your on-premises data center
    without adding public IP addresses to the Atlas access list.

To set up a private endpoint, you must have
[Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), or
[Project Network Access Manager](<Project Network Access Manager>) access to the project.

codeBlocks.content:
curl -4 -i --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
  --request POST \
  --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2025-03-12+json" \
  --data '{"providerName": "GCP", "region": "CENTRAL_US", "portMappingEnabled": true}' \
  "https://mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/<group-id>/privateEndpoint/endpointService"

sections.heading: High Availability
sections.body:
To ensure private endpoint connections to Atlas can
withstand an availability zone outage, you should deploy
subnets to multiple availability zones in a region.

sections.heading: Max Incoming Connections
sections.body:
Atlas sets the [limits for concurrent incoming connections](manual/reference/configuration-options) based on the cluster tier and
[class](storage-class-ui). Atlas connection limits apply per node.
For [sharded clusters](manual/sharding), Atlas connection
limits apply per [mongos](manual/core/sharded-cluster-query-router)
router. The number of [mongos](manual/core/sharded-cluster-query-router)
routers is equal to the number of replica set nodes across all shards.

Your [read preference](manual/core/read-preference) also contributes
to the total number of connections Atlas can allocate for a given query.

> **Example:** Your `M10` cluster has three nodes with a 1500 connection limit
> per node. Atlas reserves 10 connections per node. If you set
> your [read preference](manual/core/read-preference) to [secondary](manual/core/read-preference), Atlas
> can read from the two secondary nodes for a combined 2980 connection limit.

To learn more about connection limits and increasing your limits,
see [Connection Limits and Cluster Tier](connection-limits).

sections.heading: High Availability
sections.body:
You don't need to take additional actions to ensure that
Azure (Microsoft Azure) private endpoint connections to Atlas can
withstand an availability zone outage.

sections.heading: High Availability
sections.body:
You don't need to take additional actions to ensure that
Google Cloud private endpoint connections to Atlas can
withstand an availability zone outage.

sections.heading: Multi-Region Support
sections.body:
GCP Private Service Connect now provides multi-region support for your
Atlas clusters. You can configure [global access](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/about-accessing-vpc-hosted-services-endpoints#global-access)
to connect to private endpoints from a different Google Cloud region.
By using global access, you can ensure high availability for
your multi-region clusters, single-region deployments
hosted in a different region than your own, and Google Cloud nodes in your
multi-cloud deployments.

To learn more, see
[Introducing PSC Interconnect and Global Access for MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/developer/products/atlas/psc-interconnect-and-global-access/).

sections.heading: Max Incoming Connections
sections.body:
Per GCP Private Service Connect, a single VM can accept a maximum concurrent
64,512 TCP connections. Even if you are on a higher cluster tier
that has a `maxIncomingConnection` limit higher than 64,512,
you can have only up to 64,512 concurrent connections per private
endpoint. Google Cloud quarantines a connection for approximately two
minutes after it closes so the achievable limit will be
`64512 - (disconnect rate * 120)`. To learn
more, see [Max connections](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/about-vpc-hosted-services#max-connections) in the
Google documentation.

For more concurrent connections, you may create multiple private
endpoints for the same region.

sections.heading: Port Ranges Used for Private Endpoints
sections.body:
AWS PrivateLink supports 50 addressable targets,
Atlas can use port 1024 through port 65535,
but typically starts with port 1024. The ports
can change under specific circumstances, including
(but not limited to) cluster changes.

> **Important:** MongoDB strongly recommends using the DNS seedlist
> private endpoint-aware connection string, so that DNS
> automatically updates the ports that AWS PrivateLink uses if
> they change. For the same reason, MongoDB also strongly
> recommends allow-listing the entire port range, instead
> of specific ports.

sections.heading: Port Ranges Used for Private Endpoints
sections.body:
Azure Private Link supports 150 addressable targets. Atlas can use port 1024
through port 2524, but typically starts with port 1024. The ports
can change under specific circumstances, including (but not limited to)
cluster changes.

> **Important:** MongoDB strongly recommends that you use the DNS seedlist private
> endpoint-aware connection string so that DNS automatically updates
> the ports that Azure Private Link uses if they change. For the same reason,
> MongoDB also strongly recommends allow-listing the entire port
> range instead of specific ports.

sections.heading: Port Ranges Used for Private Endpoints
sections.body:
Atlas services are accessed through
GCP Private Service Connect endpoints on ports 1024-65535. The
ports can change under specific circumstances, including
(but not limited to) cluster changes. You may have to
update your firewall rules to allow egress traffic to the
new ports.

> **Important:** MongoDB strongly recommends using the DNS seedlist
> private endpoint-aware connection string, so that DNS
> automatically updates the ports that GCP Private Service Connect uses if
> they change. For the same reason, MongoDB also strongly
> recommends allow-listing the entire port range, instead
> of specific ports.

sections.heading: Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings
sections.body:
Dedicated Clusters

Dedicated Clusters

sections.heading: (Optional) Optimize Connection to Sharded Clusters Behind a Private Endpoint for AWS
sections.body:
Atlas can generate an optimized SRV (DNS Service Record) connection string for sharded
clusters using the load balancers from your private endpoint
service. When you use an optimized connection string, Atlas limits
the number of connections per `mongos` between your application and
your sharded cluster. The limited connections per `mongos`
improve performance during spikes in connection counts.

Atlas doesn't support optimized connection strings for
clusters that run on Google Cloud or Azure (Microsoft Azure). To learn more about optimized
connection strings for sharded clusters behind a private
endpoint, see [How can I optimize connection performance for sharded clusters using private endpoints?](optimized-connection-strings).

> **See Also:** [Connect to Atlas using a Private Endpoint](connect-private-endpoint)

sections.heading: Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings
sections.body:
When you configure a private endpoint, Atlas generates DNS
seedlist and standard private endpoint-aware connection strings:

- DNS seedlist connection

  ```none
  mongodb+srv://cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net
  ```

- Standard connection string

  ```none
  mongodb://pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net:1024,pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net:1025,pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net:1026/?ssl=true&authSource=admin&replicaSet=Cluster0-shard-0-shard-0
  ```

When a client in your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) connects to an Atlas
cluster using one of these private endpoint-aware
connection strings, the client attempts to
establish a connection to the load balancer in the Atlas VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
through one of the [interface endpoint](manual/reference/glossary).
Your client's DNS (Domain Name System) resolution mechanism handles which of the interface
endpoints the hostname resolves to. If one interface endpoint is
unavailable the next is used. This is opaque to the driver or other
connection mechanism. The driver is only aware of the hostname in the
SRV record or in the connection string.

**SRV Record for DNS Seedlist Private Endpoint-Aware Connection
Strings**

The following example shows the SRV record for an AWS PrivateLink
-enabled single-region cluster, showing three unique ports
defined for `pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net`:

```sh
$ nslookup -type=SRV _mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net

Server: 127.0.0.53
Address: 127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1026 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1024 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1025 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.
```

In the preceding example:

- `_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net` is the SRV
  record that the
  `mongodb+srv://cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net`
  connection string references.

- `pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net` is the hostname for each
  node in one Atlas cluster in one region for which
  you have configured AWS PrivateLink.

- `1024`, `1025`, and `1026` are unique ports that
  Atlas assigns on the load balancer for each Atlas
  replica set node in the region for which you enabled
  AWS PrivateLink. All nodes in an Atlas replica set are
  accessible via the same hostname, with the load balancer
  resolving individual nodes by their unique port.

**Hostname DNS Resolution in Private Endpoint-Aware Connection
Strings and SRV Records**

The hostname in the SRV record and the standard connection string
is a DNS (Domain Name System) Canonical Name (`CNAME`) record that resolves to the
endpoint-specific regional DNS (Domain Name System) name that AWS (Amazon Web Services) generates for the
interface endpoint. A DNS (Domain Name System) `ALIAS` record exists for each
subnet in your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) that you deployed the interface endpoint to.
Each `ALIAS` record contains the private IP address of the
[interface endpoint](manual/reference/glossary) for that subnet.

The following example shows the DNS (Domain Name System) lookup for the hostname in
the SRV record and in the standard connection string, including
the endpoint-specific regional DNS (Domain Name System) name for the interface
endpoint and its DNS (Domain Name System) `ALIAS` records:

```sh
$ nslookup pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net
Server: 127.0.0.53
Address: 127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net
canonical name = vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com.

Name: vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com
Address: 10.0.30.194
Name: vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com
Address: 10.0.20.54
```

codeBlocks.content:
$ nslookup -type=SRV _mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net

Server: 127.0.0.53
Address: 127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1026 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1024 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.k45tj.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1025 pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net.

$ nslookup pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net
Server: 127.0.0.53
Address: 127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
pl-0-us-east-1.k45tj.mongodb.net
canonical name = vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com.

Name: vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com
Address: 10.0.30.194
Name: vpce-024f5b57108c8d3ed-ypwbxwll.vpce-svc-02863655456245e5c.us-east-1.vpce.amazonaws.com
Address: 10.0.20.54

sections.heading: Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings
sections.body:
When you configure a private endpoint, Atlas generates DNS
seedlist and standard private endpoint-aware connection strings:

- DNS seedlist connection

  ```none
  mongodb+srv://cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net
  ```

- Standard connection string

  ```none
  mongodb://pl-0-westeurope-azure.gmmteo.mongodb.net:1024,pl-0-westeurope-azure.gmmteo.mongodb.net:1025,pl-0-westeurope-azure.gmmteo.mongodb.net:1026/
  ```

When a client in your VNet connects to an Atlas cluster using
one of these private endpoint-aware connection strings, the
client attempts to establish a connection to the Private Link
Service in the Atlas VNet through the private endpoint's
network interface. The Private Link service sends traffic through
an Azure (Microsoft Azure) Standard Load Balancer to the Atlas cluster nodes
that you deployed in that region. Your client's DNS (Domain Name System) resolution
mechanism handles resolving the hostname to the network
interface's private IP address. The driver is only aware of the
hostname in the connection string, listening on one port for each
node in the cluster's replica set.

**SRV Record for DNS Seedlist Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings**

The following example shows the SRV record for an
Azure Private Link-enabled single-region cluster, showing three unique
ports defined for `pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net`:

```sh
$ nslookup -type=SRV _mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net

Server:  127.0.0.53
Address:  127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1024 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1025 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1026 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.
```

In the preceding example:

- `_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net`

   is

  the SRV record that the connection string references.

- `pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net`

   is the hostname for

  each node in one Atlas cluster in one region for which
  you have configured Azure Private Link.

- `1024`

  , 

  `1025`

  , and 

  `1026`

   are unique ports that

  Atlas assigns on the load balancer for each Atlas
  replica set node in the region for which you enabled
  Azure Private Link. All nodes in an Atlas replica set are
  accessible via the same hostname, with the load balancer
  resolving individual nodes by their unique port.

**Hostname DNS Resolution in Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings and SRV Records**

The hostname in the SRV record and the standard connection string
is an DNS (Domain Name System) `A` record that resolves to the private IP address
of the private endpoint's network interface.

The following example shows the DNS (Domain Name System) lookup for the hostname in
the SRV record and in the standard connection string:

```sh
$ nslookup pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net
Server:  127.0.0.53
Address:  127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:  pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net
Address: 10.0.0.4
```

codeBlocks.content:
$ nslookup -type=SRV _mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net

Server:  127.0.0.53
Address:  127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1024 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1025 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.
_mongodb._tcp.cluster0-pl-0.uzgh6.mongodb.net service = 0 0 1026 pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net.

$ nslookup pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net
Server:  127.0.0.53
Address:  127.0.0.53#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:  pl-0-eastus2.uzgh6.mongodb.net
Address: 10.0.0.4

sections.heading: Private Endpoint-Aware Connection Strings
sections.body:
When you configure a private endpoint, Atlas generates DNS
seedlist and standard private endpoint-aware connection strings:

- DNS seedlist connection

  ```none
  mongodb+srv://cluster1-psc-0.4uyx2d.mongodb.net
  ```

- Standard connection string

  ```none
  mongodb://pl-0-us-east1-gcp.4uyx2d.mongodb.net:1024,pl-0-us-east1-gcp.4uyx2d.mongodb.net:1025,pl-0-us-east1-gcp.4uyx2d.mongodb.net:1026/?ssl=true&authSource=admin&replicaSet=atlas-uiq444-shard-0
  ```

When a client in your network connects to an Atlas cluster using one
of these private endpoint-aware connection strings, the client attempts to
establish a connection to the service attachments in the Atlas VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
through the private endpoints. The service attachments send traffic through
Google Cloud internal load balancers to the Atlas cluster nodes that you deployed
in that region. Your client's DNS (Domain Name System) resolution mechanism handles resolving the
hostname to the endpoint's private IP address.

sections.heading: IP Access Lists and Network Peering Connections with Private Endpoints
sections.body:
When you enable private endpoints, you can still enable access to
your Atlas clusters using other methods, such
as adding public IPs to IP access lists and network peering.

Clients connecting to Atlas clusters using
other methods use standard connection strings. Your clients might
have to identify when to use private endpoint-aware connection
strings and standard connection strings.

sections.heading: Multi-Cloud Deployment Connections
sections.body:
When you use a private endpoint to connect to a multi-cloud deployment,
you can access only the nodes hosted in the cloud service provider and region
that you're connecting from. To access all nodes in your multi-cloud deployment,
use alternative connection methods described in the
[Connections to Multi-Cloud Deployments](multi-cloud-limitation)
section in the "Configure High Availability and Workload Isolation" topic.

sections.heading: (Optional) Regionalized Private Endpoints for Multi-Region Sharded Clusters
sections.body:
For global sharded clusters that you deploy in multiple regions, if you need
to connect to Atlas using a private endpoint from networks that can't
be peered with one another, you can deploy multiple private
endpoints to a region.

You can deploy any number of private endpoints to regions that you
deployed your cluster to. Each regional private
endpoint connects to the mongos instances in that region.

WARNING: Your [connection strings](connstring-privatelink) to existing
multi-region and global sharded clusters change when you enable this
setting.

You must update your applications to use the new connection strings.
This might cause downtime.

You can enable this setting if your Atlas project
contains only sharded clusters.

You can't disable this setting if you have:

- More than one private endpoint in more than one region, or

- More than one private endpoint in one region and one private
  endpoint in one or more regions.

You can only create sharded clusters when you enable the regionalized
private endpoint setting. You can't create replica sets.

To use this feature, you must enable the regionalized private
endpoint setting.

To enable or disable the regionalized private endpoint setting:

Atlas CLI

sections.heading: Enable Regionalized Private Endpoints
sections.body:
To enable the regionalized private endpoint setting for your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes enable [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes enable](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-privateEndpoints-regionalModes-enable).

sections.heading: Disable Regionalized Private Endpoints
sections.body:
To disable the regionalized private endpoint setting for your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes disable [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes disable](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-privateEndpoints-regionalModes-disable).

sections.heading: View Regionalized Private Endpoint Settings
sections.body:
To return the regionalized private endpoint settings for your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes describe [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas privateEndpoints regionalModes describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-privateEndpoints-regionalModes-describe).

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Enable Regionalized Private Endpoints
sections.body:
Toggle the Multiple Regionalized Private Endpoints setting
to Yes.

sections.heading: Disable Regionalized Private Endpoints
sections.body:
Toggle the Multiple Regionalized Private Endpoints setting
to No.

sections.heading: Connecting to Multi-Region Clusters Without Regionalized Private Endpoints
sections.body:
If you use AWS PrivateLink and have applications that connect to
multi-region clusters that have endpoints in different regions
but are not using [Regionalized Private Endpoints for Multi-Region Sharded Clusters](atlas_regionalized-pl),
ensure that those applications can reach endpoints in the other regions.
For example, to do this with AWS (Amazon Web Services), you can [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering)
the VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s that contain the endpoints on their side.

sections.heading: Avoiding Downtime When Removing Private Endpoints
sections.body:
For multi-region clusters, you must create a
private endpoint for each region with a node.

If you're performing maintenance on a multi-region cluster, do not
alter or remove private endpoints until maintenance is complete to
avoid cluster downtime.

If you're moving from a multi-region to a single-region cluster,
you can remove old private endpoints only after verifying that your cluster
is fully functional in the new single-region setup and you've directed all traffic
through the new single-region private endpoint.

sections.heading: Billing
sections.body:
To learn more about billing for private endpoints for dedicated clusters,
see [Private Endpoints for Dedicated Clusters](billing-private-endpoints-clusters).

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- Free clusters (formerly known as `M0`) and Flex clusters do  **not**  support
  connecting through a private endpoint.

* Before you can deploy a private endpoint to a region,
  you must first resume any paused clusters
  in your project.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- AWS PrivateLink must be active in all regions into which you
  deploy a multi-region cluster. You will receive an
  error if AWS PrivateLink is active in some, but not all,
  targeted regions. If you have a multi-cloud cluster
  in AWS (Amazon Web Services) or Azure (Microsoft Azure), you must provision an endpoint in
  each provider or region and set up site-to-site VPN.

- You can do only one of the following:

  - Deploy nodes in more than one region, and have one
    private endpoint per region.

  - Have multiple private endpoints in one region, and no
    other private endpoints.

    > **Important:** This limitation applies across cloud providers. For
    > example, if you create more than one private endpoint
    > in a single region in AWS (Amazon Web Services), you can't create
    > private endpoints in Azure (Microsoft Azure) or any other AWS (Amazon Web Services)
    > region.

    See [Regionalized Private Endpoints for Multi-Region Sharded Clusters](atlas_regionalized-pl) for an exception for
    multi-region and global sharded clusters.

* To connect to Atlas clusters using
  AWS PrivateLink from regions in which you haven't deployed a private
  endpoint connection, you must peer VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s in those regions to
  VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)s in a region in which you have deployed a private
  endpoint connection.

  To learn about inter-region VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peering, see the [AWS documentation](https://aws.amazon.com/answers/networking/aws-multiple-region-multi-vpc-connectivity/).

- You can use AWS PrivateLink in Atlas projects with up to 50
  addressable targets **per region**. If you need more than
  50 addressable targets in a region:

  - Contact [MongoDB Support](request-support), or

  - Use additional projects or regions to connect to
    addressable targets beyond this limit.

  Addressable targets include:

  - Each mongod instance in a replica set deployment
    (sharded clusters excluded).

  - Each sharded cluster deployment that use
    [How can I optimize connection performance for sharded clusters using private endpoints?](optimized-connection-strings).

  - Each mongos instance for sharded cluster deployments
    that use non-[How can I optimize connection performance for sharded clusters using private endpoints?](optimized-connection-strings).

  - Each BI Connector for Atlas instance across all dedicated clusters in the
    project.

  > **Note:** To request a one-time increase to use AWS PrivateLink with
  > up to 90 addressable targets per Atlas project,
  > contact [MongoDB Support](request-support).

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
> **Important:** Some Atlas clusters on Azure (Microsoft Azure) created before 10/16/2020 use
> Azure (Microsoft Azure) networking hardware that is incompatible with Azure Private Link. You
> can still configure Azure Private Link for Atlas projects with these
> clusters to use with supported clusters in the project, but you will
> not be able to connect to the incompatible ones through Azure Private Link.
>
> All new Atlas clusters are compatible with Azure Private Link. If you must
> connect to your cluster using only Azure Private Link, you can create a new
> cluster in the same Atlas project and [migrate your data](atlas/import).

- Azure Private Link must be active in all regions into which you
  deploy a multi-region cluster. You will receive an error
  if Azure Private Link is active in some, but not all, targeted
  regions. If you have a multi-cloud cluster in AWS (Amazon Web Services) or
  Azure (Microsoft Azure), you must provision an endpoint in each provider or
  region and set up site-to-site VPN.

- You can do only one of the following:

  - Deploy nodes in more than one region, and have one
    private endpoint per region.

  - Have multiple private endpoints in one region, and no
    other private endpoints.

    > **Important:** This limitation applies across cloud providers. For
    > example, if you create more than one private endpoint
    > in a single region in Azure (Microsoft Azure), you can't create
    > private endpoints in AWS (Amazon Web Services) or any other Azure (Microsoft Azure) region.

  See [Regionalized Private Endpoints for Multi-Region Sharded Clusters](atlas_regionalized-pl) for an exception for
  multi-region and global sharded clusters.

- To connect to Atlas clusters using Azure Private Link from
  regions in which you haven't deployed a private endpoint
  connection, you must peer VNets in those regions to VNets
  in a region in which you have deployed a private endpoint
  connection.

  To learn about Global VNet peering, see the
  [Azure documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-faq#can-i-create-a-peering-connection-to-a-vnet-in-a-different-region).

- You can use Azure Private Link in Atlas projects with up to 150
  addressable targets **per region**. If you need more than
  150 addressable targets in a region:

  - Contact [MongoDB Support](request-support), or

  - Use additional projects or regions to connect to
    addressable targets beyond this limit.

  Addressable targets include:

  - Each mongod instance in a replica set deployment
    (sharded clusters excluded).

  - Each mongos instance in a sharded cluster deployment.

  - Each BI Connector for Atlas instance across all dedicated clusters in the project.

- Azure Private Link does not allow more than 64k TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) connections
  per target, which might be lower than the maximum number of
  connections that a cluster can sustain.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- GCP Private Service Connect must be active in all regions into which you
  deploy a multi-region cluster. You will receive an error
  if GCP Private Service Connect is active in some, but not all, targeted
  regions.

- You can do only one of the following:

  - Deploy nodes in more than one region, and have one
    private endpoint per region.

  - Have multiple private endpoints in one region, and no
    other private endpoints.

    > **Important:** This limitation applies across cloud providers. For
    > example, if you create more than one private endpoint
    > in a single region in Google Cloud, you can't create
    > private endpoints in AWS (Amazon Web Services) or any other Google Cloud
    > region.

  See [Regionalized Private Endpoints for Multi-Region Sharded Clusters](atlas_regionalized-pl) for an exception for
  multi-region and global sharded clusters.

- You can have up to 150 nodes when you create Atlas projects
  that use GCP Private Service Connect in a **single region**. If you need
  to change the number of nodes, perform one of the following
  actions:

  - Contact [MongoDB Support](request-support).

  - Use additional projects or regions to connect to nodes
    beyond this limit.

  > **Note:** If you have a legacy endpoint service in the same region that you have a
  > port-mapped endpoint deployed, Atlas enforces the lesser of the two limits.
  > This is either the default port-mapped limit of 150 or whatever
  > you set `privateServiceConnectionsPerRegionGroup` to be (a default of 50).

- Addressable targets include:

  - Each mongod instance in a replica set deployment
    (sharded clusters excluded).

  - Each mongos instance in a sharded cluster deployment.

  - Each BI Connector for Atlas instance across all dedicated clusters in the
    project.

- If you configure dedicated [Search Nodes](configure-search-nodes)
  for `M10+` clusters in any Google Cloud region, Atlas
  doesn't count these nodes in the total count of addressable targets.

- To request a one-time increase to use GCP Private Service Connect with up to
  200 nodes per Atlas project, contact
  [MongoDB Support](request-support).

- You can have up to 40 nodes when you create Atlas projects
  that use GCP Private Service Connect across **multiple regions**. This total
  excludes the following instances:

  - Google Cloud regions communicating with each other

  - Free clusters or Flex clusters

- Google Cloud Private Service Connect supports up to 1024 outgoing
  connections per virtual machine. As a result, you can't
  have more than 1024 connections from a single Google Cloud
  virtual machine to an Atlas cluster.

  To learn more, see the Google Cloud
  [cloud NAT documentation](https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/ports-and-addresses).

- Google Cloud Private Service Connect is region-specific. However, you
  can configure [global access](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/about-accessing-vpc-hosted-services-endpoints#global-access)
  to access private endpoints from a different region.

  To learn more, see [Multi-Region Support](private-endpoint-ha).

  > **Note:** You can't use VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peering to access private endpoints
  > from a different region.

- When you use Private Service Connect to connect to multi-region
  clusters, you can connect only to cluster nodes
  that are in the same region as the private endpoint. If
  the endpoint and the primary node are in different
  regions, you must:

  - Set your application's
    [read preference](manual/core/read-preference)
    to allow connections from a secondary node.

    For example, you can set your application's read
    preference to [secondaryPreferred](manual/core/read-preference).

  - Ensure at least one secondary node is in the same
    region as the endpoint.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To enable connections to Atlas using private endpoints, you must:

- Have a valid payment method already configured for your organization.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
- Configure the private endpoint in the same region as the Atlas
  cluster that you want to connect to.

- Have either the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) or
  [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) role in Atlas.

- Have an AWS (Amazon Web Services) user account with an IAM (Identity and Access Management) user policy that
  grants permissions to create, modify, describe, and delete
  endpoints. For more information on controlling the use of
  interface endpoints, see the [AWS Documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-endpoints.html).

- **(Recommended)**:
  [Install the AWS CLI](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-chap-install.html).

- If you have not already done so, create your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and EC2
  instances in AWS (Amazon Web Services). See the [AWS documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html) for guidance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
- Have either the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) or
  [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) role in Atlas.

- Install the [Azure CLI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azurehttps://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli).

- If you have not already done so, create your VNet and
  Compute instances in Azure (Microsoft Azure). See the
  [Azure documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/) for guidance.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
- Have either the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) or
  [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) role in Atlas.

- Have a Google Cloud user account with an IAM (Identity and Access Management) user policy and a
  [Compute Network Admin](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles#compute.networkAdmin)
  role that grants permissions to create, modify, and delete
  networking resources. For more information on managing
  private endpoints and connections, see the [GCP
  documentation](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/overview.html).

- [Install gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install).

- If you have not already done so, create your VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and
  Compute instances in Google Cloud. See the [GCP
  documentation](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-vpc.html) for guidance.

- Make sure egress firewall rules permit traffic to the
  internal IP address of the GCP Private Service Connect endpoint.

- (Optional) If you enforce a security perimeter with VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
  service controls (VPC-SC), you must create ingress and
  egress rules to establish the connection between the
  GCP Private Service Connect endpoint and Atlas clusters. See the
  [GCP documentation](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/configure-private-service-connect-services#vpc-sc)
  for guidance.

- Enable [global access](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/about-accessing-vpc-hosted-services-endpoints#global-access)
  to use Private Service Connect to connect to Atlas
  clusters in different regions.
